message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.promptquorum.com/power-local-llm/local-tts-voice-cloning-piper-coqui-xtts
**Title:** Local TTS & Voice Cloning Licenses 2026: Which Engines Allow Commercial Use (Piper, XTTS v2, F5-TTS, Coqui)
**Found via:** academic/benchmark — MOS, license, model size search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- cloudflare-one: Guides Cloudflare One Zero Trust and SASE work across Access, Gateway, WARP, Tunnel, Cloudflare WAN, DLP, CASB, device posture, and identity. Use when designing, configuring, troubleshooting, or reviewing Cloudflare One deployments. Retrieval-first: use current Cloudflare docs/API schemas instead of embedded product docs.
- cloudflare-one-migrations: Plans migrations from Zscaler ZIA/ZPA, Palo Alto, legacy VPN, SWG, or SASE stacks to Cloudflare One. Use for migration assessments, policy mapping, rollout plans, and parity/gap analysis.
- durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- turnstile-spin: Set up Cloudflare Turnstile end-to-end in a project — scan the codebase, create the widget via the Cloudflare API, deploy the managed siteverify Worker, write the frontend snippets, validate, and persist the skill. Load this when a user asks to add Turnstile, set up CAPTCHA, protect a form from bots, or fix a Turnstile integration. Mirrors developers.cloudflare.com/turnstile/spin.
- web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the page content to analyze it.

input.prompt: Extract all factual claims about local/open-source TTS engines: Piper, XTTS v2, F5-TTS, Coqui, Kokoro, and any others. Focus on: (1) quality/naturalness (MOS scores, comparisons), (2) licenses (commercial use allowed? MIT/Apache/CPML/etc), (3) model sizes, (4) voice cloning capabilities, (5) word/phoneme timing or alignment output, (6) local inference performance (CPU, Apple Silicon, speed, RAM). Quote the exact text for each claim. Also report the publish date of the article and whether it is a vendor/marketing page or independent comparison.

content.content:
# Local TTS Engines: Factual Claims Extraction

**Publication Date:** June 15, 2026  
**Source Type:** Independent comparison guide (PromptQuorum, non-vendor)

---

## Quality & Naturalness (MOS Scores)

| Engine | MOS Claim | Quote |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| **Piper** | ~3.5 | "MOS (English)" table lists Piper at "~3.5" |
| **Kokoro** | ~4.0 | "Kokoro" row shows "~4.0" MOS |
| **Coqui TTS** | ~3.8 | Listed as "~3.8" in comparison table |
| **XTTS v2** | ~4.1 | XTTS v2 rated "~4.1" in MOS column |
| **F5-TTS** | ~4.1 | F5-TTS shows "~4.1" MOS score |
| **StyleTTS 2** | ~4.3 | "achieves the highest mean opinion score (MOS) of any open-source English TTS engine" at "~4.3" |
| **Tortoise** | ~4.2 | Tortoise listed as "~4.2" MOS |
| **Bark** | ~3.2–4.0 | "~3.2–4.0 (variable)" due to inconsistent output |
| **Human reference** | ~4.5 | "Human reference MOS: ~4.5" |

---

## Licenses & Commercial Use

| Engine | License | Commercial OK? | Quote |
|--------|---------|-----------------|-------|
| **Piper** | MIT | ✅ Yes | "MIT — fully commercial, no restrictions" |
| **Kokoro** | Apache 2.0 | ✅ Yes | "Apache 2.0" with "Yes — no restrictions" |
| **Coqui TTS toolkit** | MPL 2.0 | ✅ Yes (conditional) | "MPL 2.0 — commercial use allowed, but source code of modifications must be disclosed" |
| **XTTS v2** | CPML | ❌ No | "CPML (non-commercial)" and "there is currently no one to sell an XTTS v2 commercial license" |
| **F5-TTS** | CC-BY-NC-4.0 | ❌ No | "CC-BY-NC-4.0 (non-commercial)" |
| **Bark** | MIT | ✅ Yes | "MIT — fully commercial" |
| **StyleTTS 2** | MIT | ✅ Yes | "MIT — fully commercial" |
| **Tortoise** | Apache 2.0 | ✅ Yes | "Apache 2.0 — Yes — no restrictions" |

---

## Voice Cloning Capabilities

| Engine | Capability | Quote |
|--------|------------|-------|
| **XTTS v2** | Best quality clone | "best local voice cloning option in 2026" with "6 seconds of reference audio and it clones the voice in 17 languages" |
| **F5-TTS** | Zero-shot cloning | "clones from ~3 seconds with faster flow-matching inference" |
| **Coqui TTS** | Supported | "Provide 6+ seconds of reference audio" enables voice cloning |
| **Tortoise** | Supported | "High quality but extremely slow" for cloning |
| **Piper** | Not supported | "No voice cloning" |
| **StyleTTS 2** | Not supported | "does not support voice cloning" |
| **Bark** | Limited | "Limited" cloning; "voice presets" are "approximate — not a true voice cloning system" |

---

## Model Sizes & VRAM Requirements

| Engine | VRAM | Quote |
|--------|------|-------|
| **Piper** | CPU only | "CPU only" required |
| **Kokoro** | CPU / 1–2 GB | "CPU / 1–2 GB" |
| **Coqui VITS** | 2–4 GB | "2–4 GB" |
| **StyleTTS 2** | 2–4 GB | "2–4 GB GPU. Faster than XTTS v2" |
| **F5-TTS** | 3–5 GB | "3–5 GB GPU recommended" |
| **XTTS v2** | 4–6 GB | "4–6 GB VRAM recommended for real-time inference" and "Model weights are ~2 GB" |
| **Bark** | 4–8 GB | "4–8 GB GPU" |
| **Tortoise** | 4–8 GB | "4–8 GB" |

---

## Inference Speed (CPU/Apple Silicon/GPU)

| Engine | Speed Claim | Quote |
|--------|------------|-------|
| **Piper** | Fastest CPU | "real-time on a Raspberry Pi 5" and "~10× faster than real-time on modern desktop CPU" |
| **XTTS v2** | Slow | "Slow — generates ~2× real-time on an RTX 4070" |
| **F5-TTS** | Medium-fast | "~3–5× real-time on RTX 4070" |
| **StyleTTS 2** | Medium | "~5–8× real-time on RTX 4070" |
| **Kokoro** | Fast CPU | "Fast (CPU)" in comparison table |
| **Bark** | Very slow | "Slowest of all" and "Slow — 2–4× slower than real-time" |
| **Tortoise** | Extremely slow | "Very slow (minutes/sentence)" |
| **Apple Silicon (M5 Pro)** | — | Piper "~15× real-time"; Kokoro "~8× real-time"; XTTS v2 "~3× real-time"; F5-TTS "~2× real-time" |

---

## First-Audio Latency

The guide specifies: "Piper ~30 ms (RTX 4070), ~50 ms (CPU)" and "XTTS v2 ~300 ms (RTX 4070), ~1500 ms (CPU)."

---

## Language Support

| Engine | Languages | Quote |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| **XTTS v2** | 17 languages | "generates in 17 languages: en, es, fr, de, it, pt, pl, tr, ru, nl, cs, ar, zh-cn, ja, hu, ko, hi" |
| **Piper** | 20+ languages | "20+ languages" via "downloadable voice packs" |
| **Coqui TTS** | 20+ languages | "20+ language models available" |
| **Kokoro** | English + expanding | "English + expanding" language support |
| **F5-TTS** | Multilingual | "Multilingual — strong support for English and Chinese" |
| **StyleTTS 2** | English mainly | "English mainly" and "English-only" |

---

## Word/Phoneme Alignment & Timing Output

**No claims found.** The article does not discuss alignment output, phoneme timing, or word-level timing information from any engine.

---

## Additional Technical Claims

- **XTTS v2 cross-lingual:** "Clone a voice in one language, generate speech in... 14 other languages from the same 6 seconds of audio"
- **Bark unique feature:** "only local TTS that generates non-speech audio" including "laughter, coughing, sighs, music snippets"
- **Coqui company status:** "Coqui company shut down in January 2024" but "open-source repo is community-maintained"

---

## Summary Assessment

This is an **independent, factual comparison** published June 2026 by PromptQuorum (not a vendor). Claims are supported by published benchmarks, GitHub documentation, and model cards. No legal advice is offered despite extensive license discussion.

content.text: This source provides directly relevant benchmark and license data. Let me extract the falsifiable claims.

claims.claim: On the article's MOS comparison for English open-source TTS, Kokoro scores ~4.0, beaten by XTTS v2 and F5-TTS (~4.1), Tortoise (~4.2), and StyleTTS 2 (~4.3 — highest of any open-source English engine); Piper (~3.5) and Coqui (~3.8) rate below Kokoro, vs ~4.5 human reference.
claims.quote: StyleTTS 2 ... achieves the highest mean opinion score (MOS) of any open-source English TTS engine at ~4.3; Kokoro row shows ~4.0; XTTS v2 ~4.1; F5-TTS ~4.1; Piper ~3.5; Human reference MOS: ~4.5

claims.claim: Licensing splits the candidates sharply: Piper (MIT), Kokoro (Apache 2.0), StyleTTS 2 (MIT) and Tortoise (Apache 2.0) permit unrestricted commercial use, while XTTS v2 (CPML) and F5-TTS (CC-BY-NC-4.0) are non-commercial; Coqui's toolkit is MPL 2.0 (commercial allowed but modifications must be disclosed).
claims.quote: XTTS v2: CPML (non-commercial) ... there is currently no one to sell an XTTS v2 commercial license; F5-TTS: CC-BY-NC-4.0 (non-commercial); Piper: MIT — fully commercial, no restrictions; Kokoro: Apache 2.0 ... Yes — no restrictions; StyleTTS 2: MIT — fully commercial

claims.claim: For local Apple Silicon inference (M5 Pro), the article reports Piper at ~15x real-time and Kokoro at ~8x real-time, both faster than the voice-cloning models XTTS v2 (~3x) and F5-TTS (~2x), with Piper and Kokoro being CPU-capable (Kokoro 1-2 GB).
claims.quote: Apple Silicon (M5 Pro) ... Piper ~15× real-time; Kokoro ~8× real-time; XTTS v2 ~3× real-time; F5-TTS ~2× real-time

claims.claim: On voice cloning, XTTS v2 is rated the best local option in 2026, cloning a voice from ~6 seconds of reference audio across 17 languages, while F5-TTS does zero-shot cloning from ~3 seconds; Piper and StyleTTS 2 do not support voice cloning at all.
claims.quote: best local voice cloning option in 2026 ... 6 seconds of reference audio and it clones the voice in 17 languages; F5-TTS clones from ~3 seconds with faster flow-matching inference; Piper: No voice cloning; StyleTTS 2 ... does not support voice cloning

claims.claim: The article provides no information on word-level or phoneme-level timing/alignment output for any of the evaluated TTS engines.
claims.quote: No claims found. The article does not discuss alignment output, phoneme timing, or word-level timing information from any engine.

content.text:
I've extracted the key claims from the PromptQuorum article at https://www.promptquorum.com/power-local-llm/local-tts-voice-cloning-piper-coqui-xtts.

Summary of findings:
- Source quality: secondary (independent comparison guide aggregating published benchmarks/model cards, not a vendor marketing page, but also not primary research — MOS figures appear to be compiled estimates rather than the article's own measurements).
- Publish date: 2026-06-15.
- Most relevant to the research question: a head-to-head MOS table (Kokoro ~4.0, beaten by StyleTTS 2 ~4.3, XTTS v2/F5-TTS ~4.1), a clear license breakdown (XTTS v2 and F5-TTS are non-commercial; Piper/Kokoro/StyleTTS 2 are commercially permissive), and Apple Silicon real-time-factor numbers (Piper ~15x, Kokoro ~8x, cloning models slower).
- Notable gap for the research question: the article contains no data on word/phoneme-level timing or forced-alignment output, so it does not address angle (2) of the research.

Caveat for the synthesis: MOS values are rounded single-point estimates without confidence intervals or cited evaluation protocol, so they should be treated as indicative rather than rigorous benchmark results.
